Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study in the evenings after work, but most nights I just couldn't bring myself to open the materials. When I did study, it was usually in random bursts when guilt caught up with me. I'd listen to lectures while doing other things around the house, which worked better for me than reading, but it wasn't consistent at all.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd go weeks without touching anything, then suddenly panic when I realized a deadline was approaching. Being 26 and having other responsibilities meant university often got pushed to the bottom of my priority list. The only regularity was the chaos, really.
Q3: Usually it was because I got an email notification about a deadline or because I vaguely remembered something was due soon. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything important, scroll around for a bit, and then log out without doing much. Boredom occasionally drove me there too, but that rarely translated into actual work.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd have brief periods where I'd check it more regularly, maybe after getting a bad grade or feeling guilty, but it never stuck. The mindfulness exercises they suggested helped with my stress a bit, but they didn't make me more engaged with the actual coursework.
Q5: The audio lectures were the most useful for me because I learn better by listening. I could play them while commuting or doing chores, which made it feel less like a burden. The video content was decent too, and I watched a fair amount of it, but I retained more from just listening without the visual distraction.
Q6: I barely touched the discussion boards or reading materials. The readings felt tedious and I'd zone out immediately. I also ignored most of the optional practice activities because I couldn't see the point when I was already struggling to complete the required stuff. If it wasn't going to be graded, it wasn't happening.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it directly connected to passing the exam or completing an assignment. I needed to see immediate relevance, otherwise my brain just checked out. If something felt like busywork or too abstract, I couldn't motivate myself to care about it no matter how hard I tried.
Q8: Optional activities felt completely skippable to me. There was already this mental barrier with required work, so adding optional stuff on top was just not realistic. Required activities at least had the pressure of consequences, which was sometimes the only thing that got me moving.
Q9: Yeah, I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone when I wasn't near my computer. I also had some notes from a friend who took the course before, and I relied on those quite a bit. A lot of my studying happened while I was technically doing other things, so the LMS wouldn't capture any of that.
Q10: There were times I'd listen to downloaded lectures during my commute or while cooking, genuinely trying to absorb the material. I'd also review my handwritten notes from those listening sessions. None of that showed up anywhere in the system, but it was real studying even if it looked like I wasn't doing anything.
Q11: Definitely. I'd download lecture recordings and listen to them multiple times over several days. The LMS would only show that initial download, not the five times I replayed certain sections trying to understand them. It was easier for me to engage with material offline where there were fewer distractions.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. I was dealing with work stress and just felt completely burned out. The course content started feeling increasingly irrelevant to my life, and I couldn't shake this persistent boredom. Everything felt like it required more energy than I had available.
Q13: Absolutely. When work got demanding, the LMS was the first thing I neglected. Fatigue was a huge factor too—after a long day, staring at more screens for coursework felt unbearable. Confusion about what was actually required versus optional also made me avoid logging in because I didn't want to deal with figuring it out.
Q14: So many times. I'd open videos and let them play while scrolling on my phone or thinking about other things. I'd click through materials without really processing anything. It was like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something, but my mind was completely elsewhere.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even when I knew they were important. Part of it was feeling overwhelmed before I even started. Part of it was this weird paralysis where the more important something was, the more anxious it made me, so I'd avoid it entirely. My resilience usually kicked in eventually, but often too late.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that made me engage. I'd have these last-minute cramming sessions right before something was due. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the 24-48 hours before deadlines and was nearly nonexistent otherwise. It wasn't healthy, but it's how I operated.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change much for me. By the time I got it, I'd already mentally moved on to worrying about the next thing. Sometimes negative feedback made me feel worse and less likely to engage, not more. It's not that I didn't care, I just couldn't convert that feedback into action.
Q18: The structure felt overwhelming with eight online courses happening simultaneously. I couldn't keep track of what was due when across all of them. A clearer, more streamlined structure might have helped, but honestly, I probably would have found a way to drift regardless. The flexibility meant I could always put things off.
Q19: The lack of clear priorities made engagement harder. Everything felt equally important and unimportant at the same time. What helped was when materials were available in audio format since that matched how I learn. What hurt was the sheer volume of content and the expectation of self-direction when I was already struggling with motivation.
Q20: I think those students might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching some videos, but never actually completing the work. It's easy to look busy in the LMS without producing anything. Sometimes you're just trying to figure out what you're supposed to do, or you start something and give up halfway through.
Q21: That's pretty much my situation. My exam score was decent even though my LMS activity was minimal because I studied in ways that didn't show up online. Listening to lectures offline, reviewing notes, cramming effectively before exams. The LMS data would make me look completely disengaged, but I passed.
Q22: Course logs can show when you clicked on things and for how long, but they can't show whether you were actually paying attention or learning. They miss offline studying entirely, and they can't capture the mental and emotional state behind the numbers. A student could look active but be completely checked out.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and feeling connected to the material. It's not just completing tasks—it's wanting to understand things. I don't think I was truly engaged this semester. I was surviving, getting through it, but there wasn't any real intellectual curiosity driving me.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I was struggling emotionally, or how bored and disconnected I felt most of the time. It wouldn't show the offline listening I did, or the internal battle every time I tried to make myself log in. It also wouldn't show that despite everything, I actually do want to learn—I just couldn't make it happen the way the system expected.
